Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after work, but with limited resources at home and no internet access, I had to plan everything around when I could get to campus or a café with WiFi. I would download what I could when I had connection, then study from those materials later. Some weeks I managed to put in good hours, other weeks life just got in the way completely.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I work part-time to support myself, so my schedule isn't consistent week to week. When deadlines approached, I'd scramble to find time and internet access to submit things. Regular study times were more of an ideal than a reality for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or wanting to download lecture materials while I had internet access. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone when I was near WiFi, and that would remind me to log in. The discussion posts also prompted me to check in because I genuinely enjoyed hearing different perspectives from classmates, even if I couldn't always respond immediately.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where I found a routine of going to the library twice a week specifically to use the LMS. It became almost like a ritual - I'd log in, watch videos, download PDFs, participate in discussions. But that routine broke down when my work hours increased and the stress started building up. The habit was fragile because it depended on external circumstances I couldn't always control.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because I learn best by listening. I could download them and play them back later, sometimes even just listening to the audio while commuting. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing how other students understood concepts gave me new angles on the material. Those two things together made me feel connected to the course even when I couldn't be physically present.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials, especially the longer PDFs, because reading on a small phone screen was exhausting and I couldn't always print things out. Some of the supplementary resources felt nice to have but not essential, so when time was tight, they were the first to go. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond what felt necessary because my energy was limited.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could listen to something and feel like I understood a concept better, that was satisfying. The moment something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly relevant to my grades, my motivation to complete it dropped significantly given everything else I was juggling.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had this weight to them - I knew they affected my grade, so I prioritized them even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe next week when things calm down,' but things never really calmed down. The pressure of required work was stressful but at least it gave me clear direction.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs during my limited internet time, then study from them for days without logging back in. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. My learning happened mostly in spaces the LMS couldn't see.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be listening to downloaded lectures on my phone while cooking or commuting, really absorbing the material, but the system would have no record of that. I'd also study from printed notes at home, working through problems and concepts for hours. The LMS only saw me during those brief windows when I had internet access.
Q11: All the time, yes. I'd log in once, download everything I could, then not log back in for days while I worked through those materials. The video lectures especially - I'd watch them multiple times offline, pausing and rewinding to understand difficult concepts. One login might represent a week's worth of actual studying for me.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. My work increased my hours, I was stressed about money, and the course material was getting more complex. I started feeling overwhelmed and surprised by how quickly things piled up. The CBT sessions I had helped me manage the anxiety somewhat, but the practical barriers of time and internet access remained. I felt like I was drowning even though I was trying.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I'd often have to choose between studying and earning money for rent. The confusion from falling behind made the LMS feel intimidating sometimes - I didn't even know where to start catching up.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be somewhere else entirely - worrying about bills, thinking about work, just mentally exhausted. I'd technically be 'watching' a lecture but couldn't tell you what was said. The LMS would log my activity, but real engagement wasn't happening in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was not having reliable internet to submit work when I wanted to. Part of it was feeling so behind that starting felt overwhelming. The anxiety would build, and avoiding the task temporarily relieved that anxiety, even though it made things worse long-term.
Q16: Deadlines were the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd plan my internet access around submission dates, sometimes rushing to cafés or the library at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less with the online platform. They created urgency that cut through my procrastination, but the stress of last-minute submissions was intense.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it motivated me to try harder on the next one, especially when instructors acknowledged my effort. But feedback often came late, or I wouldn't see it until my next internet session. By then, I'd already moved on mentally. Quicker feedback would have helped me adjust my approach sooner.
Q18: The course structure assumed consistent internet access, which I didn't have. Weekly modules made sense in theory, but I'd often have to batch everything into one or two sessions. The structure worked against my reality rather than with it. I appreciated the clear organization, but the pacing didn't match what was possible for my situation.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier - that was essential for me. But activities that required real-time participation or frequent logins were harder to keep up with. The discussion forums were good because I could contribute asynchronously. Timed quizzes or live sessions would have been impossible for me to attend consistently.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly active in the LMS when I could access it - watching videos, reading discussions, downloading materials. But converting that activity into submitted assignments was where I struggled. The gap between engaging with content and producing graded work is real, especially when life circumstances create barriers to completing and submitting things on time.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Someone with reliable resources might study efficiently from downloaded materials or textbooks, perform well on exams, but rarely need to log into the LMS. The platform only captures one slice of learning. I wish my exam score reflected my actual understanding better, but test-taking under stress isn't my strength.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent on the platform, which indicates something about effort and consistency. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the personal circumstances affecting a student. They can't capture whether someone was genuinely learning or just going through the motions. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, even when it's difficult. It's showing up mentally, not just clicking through pages. For me, engagement happened in quiet moments listening to lectures on my phone, thinking through concepts while working, discussing ideas with classmates in person. It's an internal commitment that external metrics can't fully measure.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my circumstances. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the stress I managed through CBT sessions, or how hard I worked to find internet access just to participate. My low attendance and incomplete assignments look like disengagement, but they reflect barriers, not lack of effort. I wanted to succeed more than my grades suggest.
